title: Glycerin and honey hand cream
subtitle: A rich, fast-absorbing hand cream with honey humectant and shea butter
excerpt: Glycerin and honey are both humectants: they draw moisture from the air and hold it at the skin surface. Shea butter adds an oil-phase barrier. Emulsifying wax binds everything into a cream rather than a lotion. A broad-spectrum preservative is essential because this formula contains water.
sourceNotes: Hand cream formulation from Gracefruit cosmetic-making guide (gracefruit.com, May 2026). Humectant action from Cosmetic Dermatology, Draelos (2010). Honey in cosmetics from Bogdanov, The Honey Book (2011). Preservative usage from Making Cosmetics guide.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Keep the preservative at exactly 1% of total batch weight.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Makes a generous jar. For gift portions, pour into smaller 30 ml tins or jars while still warm.
recipe.temperatureNote: Heat both phases to 70 degrees Celsius separately. The honey goes into the water phase at this stage: do not boil it. Add the preservative and lavender essential oil below 40 degrees Celsius.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 3 months at room temperature with 1% preservative. The honey and glycerin attract moisture from the air, which can promote microbial growth over time. Refrigerate after opening for best stability. Discard if the cream separates, smells off, or changes colour.

recipeTools.notes: For the oil phase; dedicated to cosmetic use

glossaryTerms.definition: An ingredient that attracts and retains water at the skin surface. Glycerin and honey are both humectants. They draw moisture from the air in humid conditions and from the deeper skin layers when air is dry. In a cream, humectants work best paired with an occlusive (a barrier ingredient like shea butter) that traps the attracted moisture.

content.text: Hand cream is a thick emulsion with a higher oil-to-water ratio than a body lotion. The shea butter and emulsifying wax form a substantial barrier layer; the honey and glycerin

pair work within that barrier to retain moisture at the skin surface. The result is a cream that absorbs within 2 to 3 minutes and does not leave the hands greasy: important for a functional hand cream.

This cream contains water, so a broad-spectrum preservative is mandatory. Optiphen Plus at 1% of total batch weight is the standard. Add it when the cream has cooled below 40 degrees Celsius: higher temperatures will inactivate some preservatives. The same rule applies to the lavender essential oil.

items.prepNote: The main oil-phase butter; the barrier layer
5% of batch; humectant; goes into the water phase
5% of batch; humectant; dissolve in warm water before combining phases
1% of batch; Optiphen Plus or equivalent; add below 40°C
1% dilution; add with the preservative below 40°C

content.text: Dissolve the honey and vegetable glycerine in the distilled water in a bowl over a simmering-water bath. Heat to 70 degrees Celsius. In a separate jug, melt the shea butter, emulsifying wax, and jojoba oil over the same water bath to 70 degrees Celsius. Pour the oil phase slowly into the water phase while stirring continuously. Remove from the water bath. Stir as the cream cools and thickens. When the cream has cooled below 40 degrees Celsius, stir in the preservative and the lavender essential oil. Pour into a jar and seal when fully cool.

3 months at room temperature, or 6 months refrigerated. Use a clean spatula to remove cream from the jar: avoid putting wet or dirty hands directly into the jar as this introduces microbial contamination. Discard if the cream separates, becomes watery, or develops an off smell.
